headline: Neutral Holding: Liquidity Treads Water While Credit Markets Stay Calm
body: The regime stays Neutral (score 1 out of 4) for the week ending June 10. Net liquidity — the Fed's balance sheet minus two buckets of cash that are parked and not circulating (overnight repo and the Treasury's own checking account) — barely moved, landing at $5.835T versus $5.834T last week. The 13-week trend is +0.48%, just shy of the +0.5% threshold that would flip the net liquidity signal green. Think of it like a car idling: the engine is running but you're not accelerating yet. The Fed's balance sheet itself ($6.711T) has been essentially flat since mid-May; no new injection from quantitative easing, but also no aggressive drain.

The one green signal — and the reason the overall score is 1 rather than 0 — is credit spreads. High-yield spreads (the extra interest rate risky corporate borrowers pay over safe Treasury bonds) sit at 2.78%, down 0.31 percentage points over 13 weeks. When spreads narrow, lenders are growing more comfortable with risk — a positive backdrop for equities. For context, these spreads spiked to 4.37% in April 2025 during the tariff shock; the full recovery to near-historical lows shows markets have digested that scare. The dollar (broad index 120.08) ticked up about 0.7% week-over-week, but its 13-week trend is only +0.67%, below the 1.0% threshold that would register as a headwind. A rising dollar can hurt international earnings, so we watch it, but it is not yet sending a warning.

Two structural features of the current setup are worth understanding. First, the Fed's overnight reverse repo facility (RRP) is essentially at zero — drained to nothing since late 2025. This facility was a giant parking lot for excess cash in 2023-2024; money sitting there was inert and not flowing into assets. Now empty, there is no reserve of parked liquidity waiting to be released. This is neutral, not bad, but future liquidity injections need to come from somewhere else. Second, the Treasury General Account (TGA, $0.876T) is the government's operating cash balance. When Treasury draws it down to fund spending, that cash flows into the banking system and can act as a liquidity boost — worth watching as debt ceiling dynamics evolve.

The GARP screener (which hunts for companies growing earnings 10-40% annually at a reasonable price-to-growth ratio) returned zero candidates this week out of 515 companies screened. All 515 came back with missing data, pointing to a Yahoo Finance API gap during this week's pipeline run rather than a real collapse in quality candidates. This is a known intermittent issue — treat this week's screen as a data void, not a signal. Nothing to evaluate or reveal.

The 13F tracker (which reads hedge fund quarterly disclosures to see what conviction investors are buying) could not pull data this week. The SEC's public data API rate-limits home IP addresses, returning 403 errors across all 11 tracked funds. This is expected behavior — the pipeline is designed to run from GitHub Actions IPs for this step. As always: 13Fs lag up to 45 days and cover only US long equity positions, so treat them as confirmation, never as real-time signals.
watch: Net liquidity 13-week trend is at +0.48% vs. the +0.5% threshold. One more week of modest positive drift flips the liquidity signal green and could push the regime to Risk-On (score 3+).
Treasury General Account at $0.876T. If the debt ceiling gets resolved and Treasury draws this down, it injects cash into the banking system — a potential liquidity tailwind to watch.
High-yield credit spreads at 2.78% are near multi-year lows. Any widening back above ~3.1% (the 13-week starting point) flips the credit signal neutral and drags the regime score back to 0.

Broad dollar index at 120.08, up from its February 2026 low of ~117.5. A continued climb past 121-122 would test whether the dollar is reversing its 2025 downtrend — that matters for international earnings and emerging-market flows.
change_my_mind: Flip to Risk-On: Net liquidity 13-week trend crosses +0.5% (currently at 0.48% — nearly there), credit spreads hold below 3.1%, and the dollar stays below 121. That would give all three signals green and push the regime score to 3-4, justifying full-size positions.

Flip to Risk-Off: Credit spreads widen back above 3.5% (requires a macro shock — think renewed tariff escalation, a surprise Fed hawkish pivot, or a credit event in a major sector). Alternatively, net liquidity turns negative on the 13-week basis while the dollar rips above 122 simultaneously. That trifecta — tightening credit, shrinking liquidity, strong dollar — is the triple bear signal.
